summary: The author developed a fully JIT-compilable end-to-end JAX reinforcement learning pipeline for the Lux AI Season 3 competition, extending a minimal PPO implementation with backpropagation through time and prioritized fictitious self-play. Key technical choices included designing a lightweight network architecture (~1.8M parameters) with carefully engineered unit, spatial, and scalar features, and implementing a reward schedule that transitioned from dense to sparse rewards late in training. The final submission introduced a centralized critic with global observability, which improved value estimation and doubled sample efficiency despite halving training throughput. The approach highlights the practical trade-offs between training speed, model capacity, and code maintainability in complex multi-agent RL environments.
extensive_summary:
## Approach Overview
The solution is built on a fully JIT-compilable end-to-end JAX reinforcement learning pipeline, extending a minimal PPO implementation. The architecture processes per-unit features (ally/enemy flags, visibility, energy history, position), spatial grid features (energy, nebula/asteroid fields, relic nodes, sensor masks), and scalar features (team points, match steps, deducible game parameters). The action space includes movement, no-op, and a SAP action with a 15x15 target map, masked by energy, bounds, and visibility constraints.

## Training Methodology
The core training loop employs PPO with Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT) and Prioritized Fictitious Self-Play (PFSP). PFSP dynamically weights opponent selection based on win rates against the current agent, allocating 75% of games to self-play and 25% to frozen past versions. This targeted sampling focuses training on challenging opponents to prevent stagnation. The reward schedule was initially dense (+0.002 per point, +1 per win) but switched to sparse rewards (+1 per win only) in the final week, yielding significant performance gains despite being applied to only the last 10% of training steps.

## Architecture & Experiments
The final policy network is lightweight (~1.8M parameters), as larger models consumed excessive VRAM and reduced parallel environment counts without mid-stage gains. The author experimented with replacing Conv2d with ResBlocks, adding upsampling for the SAP map, and using a Pointer Network for SAP targeting. For the second submission, a centralized critic was introduced with full global observability, requiring separate input pipelines and adding ~1.4M parameters. This improved value estimation and doubled sample efficiency, though it halved training throughput (~40k steps/sec vs 80k steps/sec).

## Lessons Learned
The author notes that while the JIT-compiled JAX pipeline maximized training speed, it severely hampered code readability and debugging. A critical bug was only caught late in the competition, underscoring the necessity of unit tests in complex RL codebases. Additionally, the author reflects that prioritizing higher model capacity (10M+ parameters) over raw training throughput would likely have produced better final results, as the centralized critic model ultimately outperformed the earlier high-throughput setup.
competition_flow: Raw game state observations are processed into unit, spatial, and scalar feature tensors, fed into a JIT-compiled PPO policy with a centralized critic, trained via PFSP and BPTT with a dense-to-sparse reward schedule, and evaluated through self-play and frozen opponent sampling to generate final submissions.
data_processing: Spatial features persist across steps with dynamic nebula/asteroid shifting; actions are masked by energy, map bounds, and visibility; team points, wins, and match steps are binary encoded.
features_engineering: Unit features (ally/enemy flag, visibility, last 5 turns energy, turns since last seen, current/last known position), spatial grid features (energy field, nebula & asteroid fields, relic nodes & points, sensor mask, visited tiles), and scalar features (binary encoded team points, wins, match steps, and deducible hidden game parameters).
insights: Switching to sparse rewards late in training yielded significant performance gains despite being applied to only the final 10% of steps.
Using a centralized critic with global observability improved value estimation and doubled sample efficiency, though it reduced training throughput.
Prioritizing higher model capacity over maximum training throughput likely yields better final results in complex RL environments.
Fully JIT-compilable pipelines maximize speed but severely reduce code readability, making extensive unit tests essential for debugging.
critical_findings: Sparse rewards applied to only the last 10% of training steps still drove significant performance gains.
Larger model capacities increased VRAM usage and reduced parallel environments without delivering mid-stage performance improvements.
what_did_not_work: Using larger models (>1.8M parameters) consumed excessive VRAM and reduced parallel environment counts without delivering mid-stage performance gains.
